result:
The clock on the wall doesn’t tick; it hums. A low, digital thrum that has replaced the heartbeat of this room. I notice it because I am trying to notice everything, a futile exercise in anchoring myself to the present. The present is slippery. It feels less like a place and more like a thin film stretched over a vast, yawning depth of what-was and what-could-be. There is a crack in the ceiling tile, a hairline fracture running toward the corner. It looks like a river on a map of nowhere.

The mind, when told to write whatever comes to it, often rebels. It becomes self-conscious, like a guest suddenly asked to perform at a party. It shuffles its feet and looks at its own internal shoes. So let’s start with shoes. My own are scuffed at the toes, a testament to a habit of leaning forward, of walking without quite lifting my feet. They are anchors, too. Without them, I would be barefoot on this linoleum, feeling every chill and every speck of dust. Sometimes I think we wear shoes not to protect our feet, but to protect the ground from the intimacy of our touch.

Outside, a bird calls—three sharp notes and a trill. A language I don’t understand but whose emotional content is clear: here I am, this is my territory, listen. We have so many ways of saying “I am here” without words. The slant of sunlight on a floor. The way a cup is left on a table, handle pointing east. The particular sigh someone makes just before sleep. These are the runes of our daily lives, mostly unread.

Memory arrives unbidden. The smell of rain on hot asphalt—petrichor—which I first consciously noticed when I was seventeen, waiting for a bus that never came. That smell is a time machine. It carries me back to that curb, to the feeling of suspended animation, of life being about to happen just around the corner. I am still, in some fundamental way, waiting for that bus. We are all waiting for buses that may or may not arrive, while the world offers us its smells and sounds and cracks in the ceiling.

I think about the people I have known who are no longer alive. Not in a sad way, just in a factual way, like noting that a book you once read is now out of print. Their voices are archived in the strange library of the mind, but you can’t check them out anymore. You can only recall their call numbers, faintly. My grandmother’s hands, which were always cool and dry, like parchment. The way my friend could laugh so hard he’d have to sit down. These are not ghosts; they are just echoes that have traveled so far they’ve become part of the background noise of the universe, the cosmic microwave radiation of personal history.

Why do we save things? A drawer in my house contains: a single chess piece (a knight), a fossilized sea shell from a beach I can’t name, a key that fits no lock I own, a ticket stub from a film I don’t remember seeing. These are the artifacts of a self. They are the proof that I passed through time and that time left some residue on me. Perhaps we are all just curators of our own minor, inexplicable museums.

Language itself is a kind of museum. Each word is a relic, worn smooth by use, its edges rounded off. The word “home,” for instance—so polished it feels warm in the hand, like a stone carried in a pocket. The word “fear” is sharper, colder. We arrange these relics into sentences, building little dioramas of meaning, hoping someone will walk through and understand the exhibit.

There is a pressure to make this “whatever comes” profound, or at least interesting. But most of what comes is not profound. It is the quiet clutter of consciousness: wondering if the plant needs water, recalling a fragment of a song (“…don’t know why I didn’t come…”), feeling the slight ache in the right wrist from too much typing, thinking about the concept of typing, how we pound letters into existence, how writing is a form of percussion.

The window shows a slice of sky. It is neither blue nor grey, but a kind of hesitant periwinkle, unsure of its own mood. A cloud, thin and frayed, moves across it like a thought drifting through a drowsy mind. The world outside the window is a narrative I am not writing. Someone else is turning those keys, driving those cars, having those conversations that start with “What do you think about…” The simultaneity of it is overwhelming. All those parallel stories, all those private museums, all those clocks humming their own low frequencies.

Sometimes I imagine consciousness as a flashlight beam in a vast, dark attic. We swing it around, illuminating a trunk here, a dusty mirror there, a pair of old boots. We never see the whole attic, just what the beam touches. And the beam is ours alone. No one else’s beam touches the same things in the same order. That’s the loneliness and the magic of it.

I remember being a child and believing that if I stared at a thing hard enough, I would understand its essence. I stared at a rock for an hour once. It didn’t reveal its secrets, but it did become more than a rock. It became a presence. That’s all any of us are, perhaps: presences staring at other presences, trying to decode the silence between us.

The hum of the clock is still there. It is the sound of this moment being measured, sliced into increments, packaged for disposal. Time is not passing; it is being consumed. And we are the consumers, eating seconds and minutes, digesting hours, leaving behind the waste product of memory.

What if we stopped measuring? What if we let time pool around us like water, and we just floated in it? The thought is lovely but terrifying. We need the ticks and hums to know we are moving forward, even if forward is just another direction in the attic.

I have used many words now. They are not the right words, or the wrong words. They are just the words that came, like the bird’s three-note call. Here I am. This is my territory, listen.

And now, the beam of my flashlight is swinging toward the end. There is a final artifact to place in the museum: this feeling, right now, of having poured something out and looking at the empty container. It is not empty, of course. It is full of the silence that follows speech, which is a different kind of fullness. The periwinkle sky has darkened. The bird is quiet. The clock still hums, a witness that won’t testify. I am here. You listened. That, for now, is enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
